title: DOM State, Events, and Accessible Interaction · State Forensics
summary: Build a keyboard-operable service-status interface whose DOM, focus, announcements, and visual state stay synchronized. Learners isolate plausible failures, compare competing hypotheses, and record cause-level repair evidence. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in an accessible interactive service-status interface and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evid…
objectives: Query, create, move, replace, and remove DOM nodes with explicit missing-element and ownership handling.
Derive DOM output from application state without duplicating conflicting sources of truth.
Trace event target, currentTarget, capture, bubble, composed paths, and cancellation before delegating behavior.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.javascript:
// javascript-basics cumulative stakeholder program.

steps.title: Order the regression proof
steps.instruction: Order the Live Updates evidence cycle for this debug task before implementing it.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Live Updates assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward.

content.text: An accessible status filter has 6 options, works by keyboard and pointer, survives dynamic list updates through delegation, restores focus after removal, and announces the changed result count. Competency probe: Announce meaningful asynchronous changes without noisy live regions or focus theft.

content.title: Ordering constraint
content.text: Order work so Live Updates has a declared contract before execution and a boundary or counterexample after it.

hints: Name the Live Updates precondition or invariant that the debug case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: An accessible status filter has 6 options, works by keyboard and pointer, survives dynamic list updates through delegation, restores focus after removal, and announces the changed result count. Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: Moving focus to every updated message is the most accessible announcement.
